blurb: The classic cut-flower pincushion — domed, honey-scented blooms in purple, wine-red, pink, and white float on tall wiry stems from summer to frost, drawing bees and butterflies and rebounding harder the more you cut.
seasons: Basal foliage rosette spring; domed pincushion flowers summer into fall; spiny green-to-tan seedheads late season; killed by hard frost
wildlife: Excellent bee, butterfly, and hoverfly nectar plant; goldfinches take the seed

care.hardiness: Grown as a half-hardy annual across the Front Range — it is not winter-hardy and is killed by hard frost, though in a mild winter or sheltered spot it can behave as a short-lived biennial. Treat it as a one-season cut-flower annual (USDA zones 4–11 as a warm-season annual; perennial only in zones 9+).[1][2]
care.planting: Start seed indoors 4–6 weeks before Boulder's mid-May average last frost, or direct-sow once frost danger has passed; transplant or thin out after hardening off. In containers it makes a fine seasonal display in a deep, free-draining pot, but the roots are frost-tender — there is no overwintering it outdoors here, so compost spent pots at season's end and resow.[2][3]
care.sun: Full sun — six or more hours — gives the most flowers and the sturdiest stems; a little afternoon shade is welcome through Boulder's hottest July afternoons, which can otherwise stall bloom.[1][2]
care.soil: Average, well-drained loamy or sandy soil; it tolerates neutral to alkaline ground (it actually likes it), so Colorado's lime-rich clay is fine as long as drainage is sharp. It will not tolerate wet feet — soggy soil brings on root rot.[1]
care.water: Moderate and even through the growing season — consistent moisture keeps the long bloom going — but let the surface dry between waterings and never leave it waterlogged. Avoid overhead watering, which spots the flowers and invites mildew.[1][2]
care.spacing: Space plants 9–12 in apart; close spacing helps the wiry stems support each other, but provide grow-through netting or brushwood support or the tall stems flop into a tangle.[2][3]
care.propagation: Grown from seed — there is no practical vegetative propagation for this annual. Barely cover the seed (light aids germination); it sprouts in 10–12 days at 65–70°F. Make successive sowings every 2–3 weeks into late spring for a continuous cut-flower supply, and it will self-sow modestly if a few heads are left to ripen.[3]
care.sow: Sow indoors 4–6 weeks before last frost or direct-sow after frost; barely cover the seed since light aids germination. Germinates in about 10–12 days at 65–70°F.[3]
care.depth: Sow shallowly — barely cover the seed, as light improves germination.[3]
care.bloom: Blooms from early summer to mid-summer and on to frost when deadheaded; the domed pincushion heads come in deep maroon-purple, wine-red, rose-pink, lilac, and white. Frequent cutting is the single best way to extend and increase bloom.[1][2]
care.feeding: Light feeder — a balanced general fertilizer at planting and again midseason is plenty. Overly rich, high-nitrogen soil grows leaf at the expense of flowers.[2]
care.maintenance: Deadhead relentlessly — this is the most important (and the most laborious) task; spent heads left on the plant cut bloom short. Cut or deadhead deeply, down between two sets of leaves, to push longer, multi-budded stems. Stake or net early before the plant flops.[2][3]
care.selfsow: Self-sows lightly if some heads are left to mature; volunteers are easy to pull or transplant and it is not aggressive. To collect seed, let the spiny heads dry to tan on the plant.[1][2]
care.harvest: As a cut flower, harvest when the blooms are nearly fully open, cutting long stems (18 in or more) deep into the plant between two sets of leaves — the deep cut signals the plant to throw more flowers. Vase life runs about 5–7 days.[2][3]
care.companions: A cutting-garden and pollinator-border staple — pair with other sun-loving annuals and cut flowers (cosmos, zinnia, snapdragon, ammi) in lean, well-drained beds.[2]

references.name: NC State Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox — Scabiosa atropurpurea
Johnny's Selected Seeds — Scabiosa Key Growing Information

edible.summary: Sweet scabious is an ornamental cut flower with no established food use — not notably poisonous, but there is no documented edible part, so don't eat it.[1]
edible.caution: Scabiosa atropurpurea is grown strictly as a cut flower and pollinator plant. Authoritative horticultural sources record no culinary use and no edible part — it is not a recognized edible herb and has no documented safe edible preparation. With no positive food record, treat it as a non-food ornamental and do not eat the flowers, foliage, or roots. (The old common names "mournful widow"/"mourning bride" and the genus name "scabious" come from historic external folk use against skin complaints, not from any food use.)[1]
